comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi jmg
For someone who is beginning the traditional golden dawn, what is the beginner supposed to use as their fist themes for meditation?
(E.g the Sephiroth, the figures of geomancy, the cosmic doctrine?)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You start with the First Meditation, which is in the first knowledge lecture -- you'll find it on p. 55 of the new hardback edition of Regardie's The Golden Dawn. Then go on to meditate on everything covered in the first knowledge lecture, giving at least one day to every symbol introduced there. Go on from there to the Pillars lecture, and then -- when you've gotten the Neophyte Grade material down cold -- go on to the Second Meditation, the second knowledge lecture, and so on. Meditate your way through all the Outer Order knowledge lectures and instructional papers, and by the time you finish doing so you'll have a more complete grasp of the symbolism of the Golden Dawn tradition than 999 out of every 1000 Golden Dawn students.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you, this helps a lot.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
In divination by sortilege (in my case Ogham) is it appropriate to assign meaning to the placement of cards based on the way I want the answer structured?

For example, I find it convenient to ask 'What would be the outcome if: I do X/ I do not do X/either,' and draw a card for each outcome. Is this meddling with the system?

Many thanks for yet another Magic Monday!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Some people find that this works very well for them, others don't have as positive a result. Give it a try and see how it works for you.

comments.title: Heathen Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I love this ritual! Thanks again, to you and Sven. I’m only a week into regular practice, but I can intermittently feel the protection, even hours after performing it. It’s as though occasionally I sense a weapon, or a star, or a sense of peaceful protection, or all of that, in my vicinity.

My husband asked me to show it to him once I had it memorized, and he said that at the end of it, he could see square-like shapes where the pentagrams were drawn. Whoa! Wish I could see that. What’s up with those shapes?

Appreciatively,
OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Heathen Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram
replies.content: Glad it works well for you. Does your husband know that he has clairvoyance? Why exactly he's seeing a square shape is a complex question, since the way astral images appear to each individual clairvoyant is a matter of subtle personal variables.

comments.title: Burying Placenta
comments.content:
I buried my child's placenta today. Me, my wife and my newborn went up to the hills. It was a foggy and windy day up there. There is a grove of bay trees there surrounded by volcanic rocks, this island in a sea of dead grass at the top of a hill. Directly below is a small labyrinth of stones. We walked the labyrinth and I left some bay nuts and my wife left a pocket knife in the center then walked out. We trekked up to the hill to the grove. We found a good spot next to a young tree then dug the hole, setting the rocks aside. We said a prayer and poured in the blood, then another prayer and placed the placenta in, we each throw a handful of soil in the hole, then buried it, turning the sod upside down on top - then we piled the stones saving one as a keepsake and stuck a dried piece of rosemary wood in the center. I tossed some soil in the bag with the blood. We said a final prayer and we walked down the hill on the other side and around a bend giving us another view of the labyrinth. On another pile of rocks overlooking the valley, I left a toy of my son's, a wooden ball. Back on the path we each grabbed a bunch of chamomile. And rubbed them as we walked back and I picked a bay leaf from the tree I took the bay nuts from and chewed it till we got back. At the car I ate an apricot and buried the pit where I had pissed when we got out. On the way back I bought a ceanothus shrub and planted it in my yard with the soil from the hill.

My hope was to show gratitude to this land as we are leaving soon. I was born here and my son too. I want him to always love this place and feel attached to it. We will be separated a month prior to leaving and I want them to return safely.

Do you have any opinions on the magical dimensions of the placenta and the ways of burying it? We kind of winged this with the little I know and a lot of hope that we stumbled on the right things to do as we had to do it soon - we left our apartment and my parent's had no room in their freezer.

replies.title: Re: Burying Placenta
replies.content: As long as you returned it to the earth in a respectful way, you should be fine.

comments.title: An odd encounter
comments.content:
I was taking down some signs from a garage sale. A homeless man that begs by the highway approached me and said "It's over?" He thought it was the next day. Then he asked if we had any religious items. Even if they are dogmatic. I walked home and got some frankincense and myrrh, a copy of the I Ching and a candle altar I got from my brother with some strange symbols I have been unable to understand. We approached his mat, he has some artwork that looks like a 3D collage on some of the poles. He was visibly excited by the incense and exclaimed "Frankincense and Myrrh!" He asked if the I Ching was a holy book as he did not permit any other texts in his realm. I gave him the altar and said I had been unable to understand it for years. He looked at the altar and said "A Burning Heart." It is an image of a heart with fire coming out of the top and a cross in the fire. He said all the other symbols were distractions. Then he said, "We blame the Masons for all their secret pass-codes and handshakes, but that is to turn people away who aren't ready." He excused himself. We shook hands and I walked home. Since then, I see this man all over the neighborhood talking to everyone. He showed up one day playing the drums by the highway. He is never there anymore, but always talking with someone.

I don't know if I have a question, just thought I should share this story. I feel drawn to talk more with this man, but I am also weary of this feeling because there is something strange and powerful about him. I said to my wife after meeting him, that he may be a saint and she told me the story of the man who was drowning so he asked God for help. A ship came by and tried to rescue him, but the man waved it on and said he was waiting for God to save him. He died and God said to him, why didn't you get on my ship.

replies.title: Re: An odd encounter
replies.content: Thanks for sharing the story!

comments.title: Magic Shamanism Organ transplants
comments.content:
Good morning Mr. Greer,

I’d like to go back to my question from last Magic Monday on improving the outcome in organ transplantation by curbing rejection episodes due to spiritual or energetic incompatibility.

You advised me to do shamanic practices if I was to approach the subject from a shamanic perspective. You also said western occultism is not shamanism and doesn’t look at the world in the same way.

My knowledge of either shamanism or western occultism is very superficial and hence my hesitation in adopting one method or the other.
The only method I’ve ever used was electional astrology, for minor surgeries.
This being a big issue I feel I need to know more.
I need a second opinion (maybe even a third).

So, without further ado,
How exactly does western occultism view this particular aspect of Health?
What rituals, planetary magic or whatever could be applied as a means to encourage better response between donor (even though he or she might be dead) and recipient?
Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Magic Shamanism Organ transplants
replies.content: I know of nothing in Western occult literature that deals with this subject at all.

replies.title: Re: Magic Shamanism Organ transplants
replies.content:
It is pretty unlikely to expect any magical tradition to deal with organ transplants since they were impossible until relatively recent times. Now, I have known someone who believed that during a nearly fatal illness his original soul was replaced by a "walk-in." I wish now that I had asked for details, but he died several years ago. I suppose something similar could happen with transplants--but it does bring up the question of what organ the soul is 'attached' to. Some cultures locate it in the heart, some in the liver, ours in the brain, or diffused throughout. So would it make a difference what organ was transplanted?

Rita

replies.title: Re: Magic Shamanism Organ transplants
replies.content: In Western occultism the soul isn't attached to an organ at all. The organs are meat -- they exist on the physical plane, period. The etheric body, which has energy centers in it corresponding to the organs, interfaces between the physical body and the soul, which is the composite formed by the astral, mental, and spiritual bodies. This is why I pointed out that Casmen900's question presupposed shamanic ways of thinking that weren't relevant to Western magic -- the assumption that some of the donor's soul remains in the organ is not something you find in Western occultism, and so you won't find Western occult methods for dealing with it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: When vibrating names, is it sufficient to do so quietly so as not to disturb others and invite awkward questions?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Very much so. It's good to practice vibrating at different volumes, so that you can do it very quietly and still get the full effect.

comments.title: How to choose a focus area
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

While reading your materials I find myself going from one subject to another. I have been working with "learning ritual magic", "druidry handbook" and "celtic golden dawn" but have not accomplished to settle into regular practice. I am reading "cosmic doctrine" and "the mystical quabalah" side by side at the moment (and notice the parallels). I use tarot daily, which is progressing very well. Recollection exercises go well. I have been unable however to do daily ritual practice and recursive meditation.

I was initially drawn to druidry but find myself studying mostly Golden Dawn material.

My questions are:
- How should one choose a focus for study? I am considering dropping the druidry material for now and work through "learning ritual magic" first, until finished. Would you recommend that?

- Given the description do you have any advice for training, in order to become more regular at ritual and meditation practice?

- How did you make choices regarding Druidry vs. Golden Dawn and how did it work out?

Regards,
Casper

replies.title: Re: How to choose a focus area
replies.content:
I'd encourage you to choose one -- it doesn't matter which one -- put the other books away, and do everything in the book you've chosen, exactly as given in the book, until you've done it all. If you can do that, you can master any branch of occultism; if you can't do that, it doesn't matter which book you choose.

I worked in the Golden Dawn tradition exclusively for better than twenty years, then spent a decade or so of hard work learning Druidry and being initiated into three different Druid traditions, then explored a range of other magical systems. Most recently I've combined Golden Dawn magic with Druid symbolism in the system taught in my book The Celtic Golden Dawn, and that's what I work with these days. I've learned a great deal from every magical system I've studied, so long as I persevered and studied all of it -- so that's what I do.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi!

Do you think someone can be reincarnated as a life form on another planet?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nope. Occult tradition has it that each group of souls incarnates on a single planet until they've learned the relevant lessons there, then moves on as a group to another planet.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'll break fairly firmly with JMG and occult tradition here: Yes. I'm not sure how common it is (it could very easily be a once-in-a-billion event), but it's possible to reincarnate on another planet - my lone past life dream pretty strongly suggests that I did so immediately prior to this incarnation. (Last incarnation's world was orbiting a red giant, which is probably part of why emigrating was possible.) My suspicion is that the method in question involved parallel universes, but I'm not sure; either way, the passageway in question had what is in hindsight hilariously obvious birth canal symbolism. (Horizontal square red brick tunnel guarded by two red supergiants. Yes, like Betelgeuse, except somehow they were also about the size of the passage.)

(It's possible that JMG's answer and mine are compatible, but if that's the case then one of the reasons for the current human population boom is that a bunch of new souls are ready for Earth's lessons and/or needed somewhere to go after their last home became unusable.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I would seriously doubt this. While it's possible to reincarnate on another planet, it's so rare as to be almost invisible. The doubts, however, have to do with the red giant - it's not physically possible to have a life-bearing planet orbiting a red giant since that's an end-of-life stage for a main sequence star, which only lasts a few hundred million years at the absolute maximum. that's not long enough for intelligent life to develop.

Transferring an entire group of souls from one planet to another in case of a cosmic (or other) disaster is possible, but it's almost always to a fresh planet that has no current sentient species, but does have one that's on the verge of sentience.

comments.title: deities, et al...
comments.content: I was wondering if you had any advice, when attempting to contact [for lack of a better term] a deity for which little is known, how to separate 'fact from fiction'. I fell drawn to Mabon, as an example, probably because so little is known, and am uncertain distinguishing projection, if not outright fantasy, from the real thing. The only thing I've ever been taught was if you get what you expect, it's probably fantasy. Thanks! Just finished Monsters-great read!

replies.title: Re: deities, et al...
replies.content: It's not easy, and it never has been -- not least because the imagination is the sensory process by which you perceive inner plane phenomena. Regular banishing rituals help, so does extensive meditation on whatever you get from a deity, but ultimately you're going to have to wing it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Jmg,

After some months, i finished the meditations on the Einingen myth.It's been a rewarding journey :)

In the Druidry handbook you ask to proceed through the material on chapter 2 before meditating on anything else. However, im very drawn to meditate on some quotes from "Where the wasteland ends" which i think are higly relevant to the Einingen myth.It's ok to spent some time on those before returning to the book material?

About an habit im picking, and not being sure if its fair:Whenever there is someone i would like to help, aside from the practical aspect, i imagine the sun above him/her with three rays descending and forming a glowing sphere around the subject, then the same for the telluric, which i imagine as a green sun beneath the earth.My intention is to bring balance and a general improvement in the subject well being and capabilities and, oddly enough, it's way easier for others than for myself.My daily ritual is way harder than this kind of visualizations.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, you can spend some time on another theme before you go on to the material in the book, if they're relevant to what's in the book. As for the visualization, are you asking for their permission before doing this for them?

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: A couple of times now when I have been meditating or praying in a meditative state with my eyes closed, I've seen an interesting pattern of light on my eyelids. The best way I can describe it is what it might look like if you had your eyes closed on a dark road and cars were driving by with their headlights on. So a light fades in slowly and then pulses and fades back out. I can feel the surge of light as well, but it is subtle. Is there any possible explanation for this or is it just a personal experience?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I love that phrase "just a personal experience" -- did you think you have any experiences that aren't personal? ;-) There are lots of possible explanations for the experience, and which one's correct is impossible to know yet. Keep up your meditations and prayers, and see what happens to the perception; it may well unfold into one or another kind of clairvoyant perception...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's well put about personal experiences; thanks for that! I'll definitely keep up the meditation and prayer and see what develops.

comments.title: Occult or mundane phenomenon?
comments.content:
Hi JMG and Allie tour knowledgeable readers.

I have a question about an experience I had over 20 years ago - which hopefully I still remember accurately, I know how memories drift over time - but that has had to be sorted into the 'Don't Know, There's Weird Stuff Out There' pile. I'm wondering if people with more occult knowledge might be able to identify the phenomenon.

A friend and I were pulling one of those all night discussions about life, the future and everything that fifteen year old girls at sleepovers tend to have. I was lying on the couch, she was sitting in a chair. She was in the middle of a sentence when she suddenly made a frightened sound and called my name. I had been staring at the ceiling listening, and so started to half turn to ask her what was wrong, when it "hit me" too.

There was a white-blue flash directly overhead and outside of the house - perhaps I could see it through the floor to ceiling windows, perhaps in my mind's eye- and I was 'thrown back' and paralysed on my back. I thought I was dying, my eyes were open to the ceiling, but I could also 'see' a dark tunnel above to the place of the flash. I felt 'pulled open' somehow. I didn't want to die, so I 'struggled', I also thought at what felt like an observer or possibly more that I wouldn't go. Seemingly seconds passed, and it occurred to me maybe it was aliens like one of those "documentaries", and I was being probed somehow. Then I wondered, maybe it was God - I was raised outside of any faith, but maybe this was what a revelation felt like. But no one said anything, showed me anything, I was just left kinda asking "who are you? What do you want?" And being 'looked at' and opened to a vastness. Then it withdrew.

My friend was still whimpering, she had her eyes closed, and as I looked she gasped and opened them. When I asked her what she saw, she said there was nothing to see, but she was pulled along through empty space, fast, and with direction changes like a roller coaster. She hadn't tried to resist or talk to anything, the movement sensation had been overwhelmingly.

We ran to the windows to look for clouds - was it lightning strike, is the what that feels like? No evidence in the house, clear winter night in the PNW.
Was it some weird effect of a helicopter from the nearby airforce base? There weren't any low aircraft in sight. Was it aliens -again, nothing to see. We had a distinct sense of feeling watched, and like reality was thin all night, but couldn't say if it was just that adrenaline hyperalert feeling.

Three friends sleeping downstairs below us, and a friend's brother upstairs, had no incidents.

I remember a man on here once saying he'd had an incident with something appearing in a tunnel in his room after his wife died, and telling him he could go see her again, and JMG agreed if he had gone, he'd have likely been found dead in his bed the next day, that there are malevolent things which would be drawn and try to prey on that grief.

Was this likely one of those things just popping by for a psychic snack?

I've always had a fear in the back of my mind that if it was supposed to be a god revelation, I blew it with my cantankerous fighting and distrust.

replies.title: Re: Occult or mundane phenomenon?
replies.content:
Since there was an Air Force base nearby, the most likely explanation is that your experience was caused by a misfired test of some sort of experimental surveillance device, or (less likely) some sort of experimental weapon system.

Other than that, yes, weird experiences do seem to happen occasionally to people, and some of them seem to be encounters with predators. Yours empathically does not sound as though you missed a chance to encounter any sort of benevolent deity or other power.

A man about three or four decades older than me (and now long passed) once told me (about a quarter of a century ago) about an experience he had had in his own youth.

Back then he was a vigorous young man with more energy than he knew what to do with, and he worked constructing houses for new subdivisions in the desert where the southern tip of Nevada touches California and Arizona. After the day's work, a truck would come to take the workers back to camp for dinner and sleep. But there were days when he still had energy to work off, so he would run back to camp by himself instead of riding the truck. He had come to know the road well, and it passed through dry and empty desert land. Once--just once!-- as he ran back to camp, he saw two trees along the side of the road, in this land that had no trees. As he ran by the trees, he glanced between them, and he saw ... not desert, but a green and verdant land, with the smell of water in its air. But the vegetation was strange and the light was strange. Though he saw no animals, there were rustlings in the vegetation, as if there were animals hiding in the vegetation. These animal sounds, too, seemed strange to him. And the land beyond the trees seemed to call to him, to pull him in.

Yet all his instincts screamed against the call from that green land, and so he kept on running back to camp. Of course, the trees were not there the next day, nor on any following day.

This man was half Native American, on his mother's side. His mother, he knew, was a person of some importance among her own people, though he never knew just why and how this was so. After he finished his work on that construction job, he went home for a rest and to see his mother; and he happened to mention the two trees and the verdant land to her. She looked at him long and hard in silence. Finally she said, "I am so glad you did not go into that land. If you had, I would never have seen you again." And not a single word more could he ever get from her by way of explanation.

Yes, there do seem to be Other Places. Some of them may be uninhabited, or even uninhabitable. Others may be homes to beings that have no interest whatever in Our Place and our small doings. Some may even be home to benevolent beings. But some of these Other Places do seem to be the lairs of stealthy predators, who may sometimes fancy a human snack or two and so cast a sort of "fishing lure" into Our Place.

If it was not something from the nearby Air Force base, then you and your friend may have been reeled in by such a predator, looked over, and then tossed back. You may have had a stroke of very good luck there.

replies.title: Re: Occult or mundane phenomenon?
replies.content: Thank you for that story. It's a small Air Force Base, and directly next to a good size town, with a commercial section nowadays, so it seems an unlikely one for hinky research. Though I suppose if they did decide to test something, the people closer by would make it easier...

replies.title: Re: Occult or mundane phenomenon?
replies.content: People have been having experiences like this for thousands of years, and I don't know if anyone yet knows for sure what they are. No, it wasn't a divine revelation; there's a great passage in the book of Isaiah, if I recall correctly, where a storm and a whirlwind and all kinds of other flashy special effects go rolling by, and those aren't the voice of God; then, finally, there's a still small voice in the silence, and that's the voice of God. It's still good advice!

replies.title: Re: Occult or mundane phenomenon?
replies.content: Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Occult or mundane phenomenon?
replies.content:
Just adding a random fact: the story is about the prophet Elijah, and it's in I Kings 19. (https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=1%20Kings+19&version=KJV).

It follows what I now read as a magical battle, with the priests of Baal dancing and slashing themselves to summon lightning.

SamChevre

replies.title: Re: Occult or mundane phenomenon?
replies.content:
That's interesting, mostly because it sounds rather similar to one of my own experiences - I'd be more confident if you'd mentioned black hole symbolism. (While ill and asleep, I was pulled through "space" to a black hole, which I orbited; my impression was that I was offered a choice to pass through, and refused on similar grounds to you.) I've also had the lingering suspicion that it might have been an opportunity, so if it's the same phenomenon that may be part of it.

Even if your experience isn't from the same phenomenon, I'd be very surprised if it's divine in nature. It's definitely not what usually gets called divine revelation, the small quiet voice (though I'm suspicious that said voice is working on a slightly different level to the gods) - I've had that experience too, twice, and it's definitely different. It's not consistent with a couple of other experiences I've had with something-like-gods, either. One possibility given your description (which I don't think is in play for my experience) is that you got the equivalent of picking up an ant, looking at it, and putting it back down. Alternately, it could be a malevolent entity that needs your consent to do nasty things to you - compare, well, the same JMG comment you remember.

My experience was nowhere near an Air Force base, so if it's the same phenomenon (not guaranteed) I'm dubious about military experiments being a cause, though I can't rule it out entirely since I think the location of my experience is on an occasional flightpath for military choppers. Was there holy ground in the area, or somewhere where the walls of reality are *usually* thin? That definitely applies in my case. (I suppose "military experiments weakened the fabric of reality" could be in play...)

replies.title: Re: Occult or mundane phenomenon?
replies.content:
Thanks for that story, my friends experience sounded a lot more like being taken to your black hole, perhaps I interpret before she got there.

"picking up an ant, looking at it, and being put back down".

That very much was what it was like. I wasn't explicitly offered a choice in anything. Hard to say if there was holy ground around - the house is up on a hill, there is a stream nearby, below, and down the other side of the hill, the ocean and a lagoon, so it may well have been important to First Nations historically. It always felt different there. That friend's parents had also collected a number of articles from tribes in Papua New Guinea, some of which were used in shamanic ritual, so they could have inadvertently set up a welcome mat.

comments.title: Color Breathing
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Re: Color Breathing

Let's say during a particular session my goal is to focus on green. Yet I keep seeing yellow, or yellow just seems easier to imagine and -- for lack of a better word -- easier. As if there is less resistance.

Does this tend to mean there is an imbalance, and I need to continue to work on visualizing green? Or does it mean I'm somehow "craving" yellow and need to go with the flow?

replies.title: Re: Color Breathing
replies.content: Experiment with it. Do a bunch of yellow color breathing and see if that seems to fill the need. Also try pushing back and getting green even when your mind wants yellow, and see what results you get.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
That is fascinating. No, clairvoyance is news to hubby. Almost 25 years ago, he and I stood in a lovely public garden on Whidbey Island, admiring the rhododendrons, when I looked over at him and saw quite clearly that he stood within the body of a young Asian man, in monk robes. I feel quite certain that was one of his past lives. If so, would his training as a monk long ago have anything to do with the shape of what he saw?

He’s not religious per se, but is totally accepting of my lifetime of seeker adventures. I’m quite lucky he’s my mate.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Very likely, yes, the monastic training he received in that previous life will have opened up specific patterns of clairvoyance in him, which would carry over from life to life.

comments.title: differences between divination sources
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I hope that it's not the quantity of responses to comments and questions on your blogs that's making you tired. I'd hate to be part of the problem and wish you plentiful r&r.

I wonder if you might let me know if this is a good way to handle discrepancies between divination meanings. I'm new to working with Ogham, currently only have access to two texts that relate meanings if the fees and have found the correspindences for two fews are transposed. Until I can find more sources, I've decided to just stick with one source's interpretation for now.

Or would you recommend something else?

Many thanks!

replies.title: Re: differences between divination sources
replies.content: Choose one, stick with it, and see what results you get. Every divination system in existence has variations like this -- in the Tarot, for example, there's been a long dispute as to whether Justice should be trump 8 and Strength trump 11, or vice versa.

replies.title: Re: differences between divination sources
replies.content: Excellent, thank you for this and for tolerating my ridiculous typos!

comments.title: nesting rituals
comments.content:
Hello JMG.

There has been some discussion of ways to perform rituals when in public spaces. I have a related question.

I have been doing the LRP and Middle Pillar practices together at home.
As I understand it - please correct me if wrong - the order is:
Circle of Presence
LRP
MP exercise
Circle of Presence

The LRP (done without the MP) is bracketed before and afterwards by the CoP.

Now if there is time only for the LRP, and I wish to continue with the MP and meditation later in the day, must the MP exercise also be bracketed by the Circle of Presence, or preceded by the entire LRP, or neither? i.e. I'd like a clearer understanding of the relations between the functions of these three. I have not any perception yet as to what they are doing and the logic of the parts.

Thanks as ever,

kallianeira.

replies.title: Re: nesting rituals
replies.content: It's best to do the whole thing as a unit; the LRP, which begins and ends with the Circle of Presence, and then the MP, with the Circle of Presence following it. Both the ritual and the exercise should always begin and end with the Circle of Presence -- when you do them together, the Circle of Presence that ends the LRP begins the MP, so you only do it three times.

replies.title: Re: nesting rituals
replies.content:
Hi JMG,

Just want to say thanks for the very clear answer to my question.
I hadn't realised that about the CoP being in there three times.
I do hope it's not impertinent to send this reply on Tuesday (it is Wednesday here already)!

kallianeira

comments.title: MIght be considered a question about magic...
comments.content:
How does a druid deal with mosquitoes when outside practicing rituals in the summer?

Here in the woods in central Wisconsin they are terrific and I'm often driven indoors from the innumerable swarms. I stay covered head to toe with clothing and a mosquito net over my head. It keeps about 80 out of 100 of them from biting but they still becomes such a distraction from my practice.

I'm of the mind that this is their home as much as mine and I'm no fan of bug-bombing and citronella candles and herbal sprays and such can't handle the sheer number of them this particularly overwhelming mosquito season.

I'm curious if you've dealt with or overcome this relentless insect.

replies.title: Re: MIght be considered a question about magic...
replies.content: I use herbal oils, and lots of them.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What determines where ley lines form? Do standing stones alter the path of leys or just make the most of them as they are?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There's been a lot of confusion and nonsense written about this recently, so I appreciate the question. Leys aren't natural phenomena -- they were created some thousands of years ago, in some parts of the world, using processes of magical engineering that nobody knows any more. As any feng shui master will tell you, when the natural energies of the landscape are left to themselves, they'll move in complex curves, not in straight lines!

The standing stones in those places that have leys -- far northwestern Europe, New England, Japan, and a few other places -- are an integral part of the ley structure. So are mounds, and some other works of geoengineering. Again, we don't know more than a few scraps about how leys were made and how they were used, but it's pretty clear that (for example) when a standing stone falls over or is removed, the flow of energy through the associated ley changes.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: When you do colour breathing and breathe out impurities, what do you visualise happening to them after that? If you just breathed them out and they were still in the vicinity I'd feel like there was a danger of reinhaling them on the next breath.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I visualize them dispersing outward into infinite space and being transformed into light.

comments.title: Kunstler’s Magic
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I’ve been wondering if you might share any thoughts on the magical depictions in the World Made By Hand series. The title character in The Witch Of Hebron comes to mind, but also Brother Jobe’s unusual abilities. As someone who found both you and JHK through the Peak Oil scene I was surprised to find that element in his books, and curious as to the reaction from someone who is magically informed. Thank you.

P.S. Rather ironic that his Peak Oil fiction involves the occult, while Retrotopia prominently features street cars and good city design!

replies.title: Re: Kunstler’s Magic
replies.content: I thought it was interesting, and not at all bad for something written by someone who hasn't studied or practiced magic.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi again,

About a day or so after my grandma died, my mom swore that my grandma called her name. My mom was fully awake when this happened. Is such a thing possible, so soon after someone dies?

Thank you again for your time, it's so interesting to read all of the questions and answers.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's the most common kind of ghost experience there is. Something like a third of all people have had an experience like that -- my dad, who insists that he's one of the least spiritual people alive, heard from my grandmother after her death in exactly the same way. (I got a phone call from a very, very spooked father right afterwards!)

During the three days or so immediately after death, the spirit is still very close to the material world, and it often happens that it will try to reach out and make contact with the people it's left behind, to comfort and reassure them. After that, it passes through what's called the Second Death, and has other things to occupy its time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It happened to me two months after my mother died and it happened to my husband, who isn't very spiritual, years after his grandmother's death - she spoke to him in a moment of physical danger. He was very close to her.

comments.title: A Ritual for Concentrated Intellectual Work
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I wondered if you might have a suggestion for a simple ritual for focusing on intellectual work? I am employed as a civil engineer, I find that I often have trouble focusing, but that sometimes I find myself in a state of deep engagement, and would like to understand and make that state re-producible. Those various states all occur on the same subject/project, so I don't think it is the content that is preventing me from focusing, but rather an imbalance or untrained aspect in my self.

The first thing that jumped to mind is a ritual of some sort, and you seemed a good person to ask.

I am not embedded in any particular tradition, though I am European-descended and Western symbolism tends to resonate more frequently than Eastern for me, if that helps you point me to a particular ritual.

I do not have any of your books on ritual, but I will happily buy one if you have already detailed such a ritual there.

Thanks for your generosity in offering these Magical Monday's!

DutyBound

replies.title: Re: A Ritual for Concentrated Intellectual Work
replies.content: Magical ritual doesn't work like that. It's a little as though you asked me to teach you a Bach fugue for violin when you've never studied violin and don't know how to read music. Magic -- at least the kind of magic I do -- is like playing a classical instrument; it takes the same kind of regular practice, and you have to invest a lot of time into it before you can play anything much more complex than "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Pentagram," but the more you practice it the more you can do.

replies.title: Re: A Ritual for Concentrated Intellectual Work
replies.content:
A very informative response - thank you.

May I ask a clarifying question - would you say that magic would be an appropriate tool to pursue what I described? I understand that it will take me a long time to actually be able to see significant gains, but it would help to know that I am heading down a path that has a good chance of going where I would like to.

DutyBound

comments.title: (no subject)

I've been educating myself about fences, predator scents, and rabbit-resistant plants, and am looking for magical ways to back those up by making rabbits vaguely uncomfortable.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Good question. I don't know of a spell for that purpose, though cooking and eating as many of the rabbits as you can catch might be a good start. ;-)

comments.title: books on astrology
comments.content:
Hi JMG

I've had discussions with a good friend of mine regarding belief in astrology. He is quite smart but also skeptic. I tend to fail at giving sound arguments about how astrology works, and I wanted to know if you could reccommend me (or him, for that matter), some books that can reconcile these beliefs with someone who is open to it, but hasn't found the evidence to back it up.
Thanks!

replies.title: Re: books on astrology
replies.content: I've never really looked into books like that. My approach to astrology was simply to give it a fair try. I found that it worked very well, and proceeded from there.

replies.title: Re: books on astrology
replies.content:
I don't know of any books on astrology, but here's a tale you can try on your skeptical friend. In considering any great decision, there are many different aspects or factors to consider. How will it affect your family? Your neighbors? Your environment? Your short-term goals, and your long-term goals. etc, etc. If you associate these factors with the sun and its planets, you create a structured way to plod through the factors as you consider each heavenly body. Throw in some arcane calculations, and you slow down your conscious brain even more, so the unconscious brain has time to draw deep associations. When you're done with the astrological procedure, you're in a much better position to make a good decision, and (being DONE with the procedure) it's time to MAKE the decision. Not to decide on the act is to decide not to act, and often any plausible action produces a better outcome than no action at all.

In a way, I think of it as a much more complex version of other thinking structures: "five whys", or "strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, dangers".

You don't need to worry about believing that the planets have some gravitational or electromagnetic influence; just follow the procedures and let your unconscious [sic] be your guide.

comments.title: General question
comments.content:
I've read a couple of your books about magic, most recently the book about natural magic, and I'm having a bit of trouble piecing things together. I hope I can word this question so that you understand what I'm looking for.

Back when I was attending a Pentecostal church as a teen, the practices of the religion: worship services, speaking in tongues, the many forms of prayer, "slaying in the spirit", all made sense because there was a background belief system of which they were an integral part. One understood when and how to use these separate elements, because one understood the fabric into which they were woven. I didn't always agree (hence I left in my 20's), but it made sense as a whole.

What I can't seem to wrap my mind around is a coherent belief system in which the elements of natural magic are a part, something that would tie all of it together. Am I looking for something that is not there? It's as if the book of natural magic is a collection of recipes, wonderfully useful for someone who understands the basics of cooking and menu planning, but confusing to someone who does not.

Beekeeper in Vermont

replies.title: Re: General question
replies.content: The first chapter or so of the book, pages 3-14, covers the worldview and philosophy of natural magic; you can also consult my book Circles of Power, which gives a more lengthy discussion of the magical worldview.

comments.title: book, sharp object
comments.content:
1) I just recently received 'The Encyclopedia of Natural Magic'. Too good a deal to pass up. And after reading just a few paragraphs of the introduction I'm even happier that I ordered it - looks to be fascinating and practical. Thank you!
2) Re: sharp object near TV. Inconclusive so far. Along with other items, the sharp objects (which vary) left near the TV seem to disappear. Hubby, who knows nothing about the experiment & is the one who wants the TV (and keeps it on), may be sub-consciously clearing things aside. Will keep trying. As for me, I'd prefer not to have a TV, but if it's there it can be distracting especially if there is something of interest being aired (not much). But I've found it easier to read or do crafts when the sharp object has been in place.

replies.title: Re: book, sharp object
replies.content: Many thanks for the enthusiasm and the data points!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Do you have an opinion on the subject of assigning elements to the four directions? I’ve seen every possible combination suggested at some point, save that Northern Hemisphere systems seem disposed to put Fire in the south. I realize that part of the answer is necessarily ‘each system makes sense in terms of its own internal logic,’ but what I’m asking is if you favour one such internal logic over another.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I use the standard Golden Dawn version -- air in the east, fire in the south, water in the west, earth in the north. My sense is that all the systems are arbitrary, but it works best to choose one and stick with it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John--

When I am in my regular meditation, I often allow my hands to move about as they feel guided (by the nwyfre, I'm assuming). I've discovered that even when my eyes are closed, I can still "see" my hands, like blobs of dark luminescence (?) -- it isn't light, but it is a different darkness than the background darkness of the inside of my eyelids. I've experimented a bit with this, to see if what I'm experiencing is real: watching my hands as the move, moving them is certain directions, etc. When I bring my hands up by the sides of my head, I get this sensation of a light being shone in the corners of my vision. (Again, light that isn't light, but feels like light.) I've also noticed that this intensity "builds" over the course of the meditation.

Does any of this make sense? Am I "seeing" nwyfre when I'm experiencing this? Or is this something else?

--David, by the lake

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It could be any number of kinds of clairvoyant perception. The only way to find out exactly what it is is to keep practicing and see how it develops. You may be seeing nwyfre, you may be seeing things on any number of planes of being -- you'll just have to, er, see.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you, John. I'll keep working with it and see where it all goes.

David

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
John,

It is very helpful that you field these questions, so thank you again for that. I'm trying to orient myself in this world I have found myself in and that can be a challenge at times. My daughter has assured me that I'm not crazy, so there's that, at least. Of course, she's something of an intuitive occultist, whereas I'm having to peel back decades of rationalism. I'll get there.

David

comments.title: Reading exercise in Learning Ritual Magic
comments.content:
Hi John,

I've been working with Learning Ritual Magic for few months now.

One of the books I choose for the reading exercise was Paths of Wisdom, and now I got to the part dedicated to the symbolism of the Tree (chapters 7-9). These chapters are much longer than the others (like 60-70 pages instead of 10-15), so I'm finding it difficult to choose only 7 sentences for each chapter. Do you think it would be appropiate to choose more than 7 sentences and spend 3-4 weeks for each chapter?

replies.title: Re: Reading exercise in Learning Ritual Magic
replies.content: With Circles of Power, I'd recommend choosing seven sentences from each of the sections describing the Spheres, and at least three sentences from each of the sections describing the Paths. As you've noted, those are hefty chapters with a lot of material in them, and so it makes sense to quarry them more thoroughly.

comments.title: Yeats the Magician
comments.content:
JMG,

Speaking of Yeats...

I have tried to find out more about his involvement with the Golden Dawn and other mystical and magical streams. I have read several online sources as well as a couple book sources. I learned a few things, but nothing I encountered had great depth. That is, what I read didn't seem to be written by a magical practitioner. Therefore, there wasn't much insight into the how's and why's of his magical regime.

I am wondering if you could point me to any good sources exploring Yeats's magical life and/or if you might share a bit of what you know of his magical interests and endeavors.

Thanks much,

Pierre

replies.title: Re: Yeats the Magician
replies.content:
Good heavens, there's a huge literature on Yeats' Golden Dawn involvements! I'd start out with Graf's W.B. Yeats: Twentieth-Century Magus, Harper's Yeats's Golden Dawn, and Raine's Yeats the Initiate. You might also look over the bibliography here, which covers the ground very thoroughly.

Yeats himself wrote that magic was, next to poetry, the great passion of his life, and he put a vast amount of time into magical study and practice, from his teen years right up through old age.

replies.title: Re: Yeats the Magician
replies.content: Thanks.

comments.title: Fusion of East and West
comments.content: Are you aware of any successful magical systems that fuse eastern and western traditions coherently with more than simple borrowing of bits and pieces? You've said before that you've drawn inspiration from the east but also mentioned some conflicts while practicing Qi Gong with western occultism. What is the primary conflict between this type of synthesis? Is it that no one has tried or that they did not know both systems in full before attempting to combine them?

replies.title: Re: Fusion of East and West
replies.content:
We're in the very earliest phases of working out meaningful combinations of Western and Asian occultism, and of course one major problem is that our Western occult traditions are in such fragmentary form after the long night of Enlightenment rationalism! The problems I and others have had with combining Western and East Asian practices are that the effects on the body's energetic balance of combining practices are unpredictable, and can be problematic. In my case, the combination gave me a bad case of depleted kidney qi, with the usual range of serious health problems that involves, and those didn't go away until I dropped all my taijiquan and qigong practices.

Five or six centuries from now we'll probably have a good handle on what you can do safely and what you can't. The big problem seems to come from any combination of the exercises that run life energy (prana, qi, nwyfre, etc.) through the body. Messing that up can quite literally kill you -- Manly P. Hall wrote in a couple of places about what happened when a bunch of healthy young Californian Theosophists he knew set out to practice kundalini yoga out of the books by Arthur Avalon aka Sir John Woodroffe. The short form is that they turned pale and weak, and one after another, dropped dead. Not a good outcome for a spiritual practice!

comments.title: Words
comments.content:
Do the words "esotericism" and "occultism" have different meanings or connotations in English? Or in general? Sometimes people make a distinction between them, calling the modern phenomenon "occultism", while esotericism is presumably the historical tradition. This is something of a grammar Nazi question, but I did notice that you often use "occultism"...

Tidlösa

replies.title: Re: Words
replies.content: I use the term "occultism" because it was invented by Eliphas Levi, the founder of modern ceremonial magic, to refer to the kind of esoteric philosophy and magical practice I engage in. My take is that by and large occultists call what they do "occultism," and academics tend to call what occultists do "esotericism," but your mileage may vary.

comments.title: Question from last week.
comments.content:
Archdruid,

Last week I asked, “I have a young cat who is a little naive and not very aggressive. He also has feline leukemia, and I worry that if he ever gets out of the house he wouldn't make it. I want to cast a protection spell on him, do you have any advice as to how to do this? Should I ask his permission first?”

You responded, “What kind of magic do you know how to do?”

I’m practicing out of the Druid’s handbook, I’ve been doing the SoP twice a day consistently for two years. Well more or less consistently, I only miss practice when I’m hungover. I’ve done one scrying excursion into my mental plane, and successfully cast spells to improve my financial status, relationship status, and ability to study consistently. I’m also studying the Philosophy of William Walker Atkinson and Dion Fortuna. I am not practicing with any other system other than the one you've developed in the Druid's Handbook and the Druid Magic Handbook.

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: Question from last week.
replies.content:
Okay, in that case you should certainly ask the cat's permission. Imagine very clearly what you want to do, and ask him if it's okay; then pay attention to whatever response he makes. It may be very clear!

If he says yes, use one of your two daily SoP practices each day for at least seven days to bless your cat. When you do the invoking phase of each element, invoke blessings on the cat, and when you do the banishing with the four material elements, banish all harm that may come to him from air, from fire, from water, and from earth. That should do it.

replies.title: Re: Question from last week.
replies.content: That seems like a simple enough tool that could come in handy once in a while. For curiosity's sake, how could I know if I am ready to try it?

comments.title: Prophecy
comments.content:
Are "real" prophecies of the future possible, as in some god or spirit actually revealing future events in advance, as in the Bible, where some prophets (supposedly) predicted the fall of Jerusalem, the Babylonian captivity, and so on. I believe you said once that physical events "happen" in the astral before they do so on our plane of reality, so could a prophet (or god) somehow "see" this in visions and hence "predict the future"?

Tidlösa

replies.title: Re: Prophecy
replies.content: You'll have to ask a prophet or a god. I'm neither, and haven't spent a lot of time around either prophets, on the one hand, or gods who like to predict things, on the other, so I honestly don't know.

comments.title: Pessimism
comments.content:
This is something of a pessimistic question, but do you believe that all humans will make it to the next evolutionary-spiritual level? I recall you said that Earth was a testing ground for souls until they learn certain lessons, also that souls tend to incarnate in groups, but what if some soul or even group of souls simply fail to learn? Can a soul or "monad" be destroyed?

Tidlösa

replies.title: Re: Pessimism
replies.content: It's the traditional faith of the Druid Revival that every soul, no matter how pig-headed, will eventually make it to Gwynfydd at last. Souls can certainly fail to learn, but when they do, it just hurts more. And more. And more...

Sandalphon-sprit-below, Metatron-sprit-above.

I've started reading the art of memory at your recommendation.

Thank you for your patience with all of us.

replies.title: Re: Relationships with angels.

replies.content: That's interesting. My first home altar was a two drawer filing cabinet made of black steel. Its proportions were similar to what you specify. I used it for devotions or magic almost nightly for a couple of years, and it picked up a heck of a charge.

comments.title: Supernatural experience
comments.content:
(Tolkienguy)

The conversation above about supernatural experiences reminded me of something in my own family. About four years ago, my grandmother died, and for months afterward, my grandfather claimed he could still hear her voice in the house, calling his name. Nobody else in the family heard it, and pretty much everyone except me took it as evidence of grandpa's senality. When I told my sister that I thought it might actually be grandma's ghost, she asked why grandma "didn't haunt the rest of us". What do you think it was? Is there anything in occult lore about this sort of thing? Grandma and grandpa were married for about 60 years-could that be a reason he heard it and no one else did?

replies.title: Re: Supernatural experience
replies.content: That kind of thing happens all the time. Your grandparents will have had fairly close etheric connections after living together all those years, and it may have taken your grandmother longer than usual to go through the second death.

replies.title: Re: Supernatural experience
replies.content: I suspect this sort of after-death visit happens far more often than it doesn't.  It's just that people don't mention it.  I've heard so many stories, simply by asking the widow(er), "So did s\he show up after the funeral to say goodbye?"
